cue: How do you say things went from bad to worse?

es.literal: to walk from Herod to Pilate
es.meaning: To go from one bad situation to a worse one; to be passed around fruitlessly.
es.example: Con esos trámites ando de Herodes a Pilato.
es.exampleTranslation: With this paperwork I'm sent from pillar to post.

it.literal: to fall from the frying pan into the embers
it.meaning: To go from a bad situation to a worse one.
it.example: Con quelle pratiche sono caduto dalla padella nella brace.
it.exampleTranslation: With that paperwork I went out of the frying pan into the fire.

bridge.note: Same worsening, two very different registers of misery. Spanish sends you on the Gospel journey of Christ's trial — shuttled from Herod to Pilate, from one authority to the next, nobody taking responsibility: a bureaucratic hell with a biblical face. Italian drops you from the frying pan into the coals — pure kitchen physics, hot to hotter. English keeps the Italian pan but swaps embers for fire. Scripture versus stove for the identical predicament.

interference.es_to_it: The Italian is culinary, not biblical — dalla padella nella brace. 'Da Erode a Pilato' would only be understood as the literal Passion route.
interference.it_to_es: 'De la sartén a las brasas' is understood but is not the fixed Spanish idiom, which walks the Passion: de Herodes a Pilato.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Scripture-vs-stove; note this is a THIRD 'bad to worse' entry (0099 estacada is unrelated; salir de Guatemala queued). Kept for the biblical/culinary contrast.
